/* ============ NO CHANGE!! ============ */
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Roboto:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&display=swap');


/* ============ CONTACT FORM POPUP BG ============ */
#popmake-2374878 {
  background-image: url("https://old.thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/backnew.png") !important;
  background-size: cover !important;
}


/* ============ HUBSPOT FORMS ============ */
.popupForm form {
  display: grid;
  justify-content: center;
}

.popupForm .hbspt-form .actions {
  text-align: center;
}

.popupForm .hbspt-form .actions input[type="submit"] {
  width: auto;
  padding: 0 32px;
}

.hbspt-form label span {
  font-family: "Roboto", Sans-serif;
  font-size: 12px;
  font-weight: bold;
  line-height: 20px;
  color: #fff;
  margin: 0 0 0.5em 0;
}

.hbspt-form .hs-form-required {
  color: red !important;
  margin-left: 3px;
}

.hbspt-form input:not([type="submit"]):not([type="checkbox"]):not([type="radio"]),
.hbspt-form select {
  font-family: "Roboto", Sans-serif;
  font-size: 14px;
  font-weight: 500;
  color: #ffffff;
  background-color: #31354A;
  border: 1px solid #4F556B;
  border-radius: 5px;
  padding: 5px;
  width: 100%;
  height: 45px;
  margin: 0 0 0.5em 0;
}

.hbspt-form input:not([type="submit"]):not([type="checkbox"]):not([type="radio"])::placeholder {
  color: #50566c;
}

.hbspt-form .hs-input.hs-fieldtype-intl-phone select {
  font-size: 10px;
  width: 35% !important;
}

.hbspt-form .hs-input.hs-fieldtype-intl-phone input {

}

.hbspt-form .actions input[type="submit"] {

  font-weight: bold;
  color: #ffffff;
  background-color: #0AA0AB;

  border-radius: 5px;
  width: 100%;
  height: 50px;
  line-height: 0;
  padding: 0;
  margin-top: 4px;
  box-shadow: 0 0 10px 0 #0AA0AB;
}

ul.no-list.hs-error-msgs {
  font-family: "Futura", Sans-serif;
  font-size: 12px;
  list-style-type: none;
  padding-left: 0;
}

ul.no-list.hs-error-msgs label {
  color: red !important;
  margin-bottom: 8px;
}

.hbspt-form .hs_error_rollup {
  margin-top: 8px;
}

.hbspt-form .submitted-message {
  color: #fff;
}


/* ============ DOCUMENT FORM RESPONSIVE ============ */
@media (max-width: 556px) {

  }

.search-field {

  font-family: 'Roboto' !important;

  border-bottom: 1px solid #000 !important;
}

.search-submit {
  background: transparent !important;
  border: 1px solid !important;
  color: #000 !important;
  font-size: 14px !important;
}


/* ============ AUTHOR PROFILE + SOCIAL SHARE ============ */
.tqi-profile {
  padding: 22px 16px;
  font-family: Futura, "Futura PT", "Trebuchet MS", Arial, sans-serif;
}
.tqi-profile__inner {
  max-width: 760px;
  margin: 0 auto;
  text-align: center;
}
.tqi-profile__avatar img {
  display: block;
  margin: 0 auto 12px;
  border-radius: 9999px;
}
.tqi-profile__name {
  margin: 0 0 8px;
  font-size: 24px;
  font-weight: 700;
}
.tqi-profile__bio {
  margin: 0 auto 14px;
  max-width: 720px;
  font-size: 14px;
  line-height: 1.6;
}

.tqi-profile__social {
  display: flex;
  justify-content: center;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  gap: 10px;
}
.tqi-social {
  display: inline-flex;
  align-items: center;
  justify-content: center;
  padding: 9px 12px;
  border-radius: 10px;
  text-decoration: none;
  color: #fff !important;
  font-weight: 700;
  font-size: 13px;
  line-height: 1;
  transition: opacity .15s ease;
}
.tqi-social:hover { opacity: .92; }

.tqi-social--twitter   { background: #000000; }
.tqi-social--facebook  { background: #1877F2; }
.tqi-social--instagram { background: #E4405F; }
.tqi-social--linkedin  { background: #0A66C2; }
.tqi-social--youtube   { background: #FF0000; }
.tqi-social--reddit    { background: #FF4500; }
.tqi-social--telegram  { background: #26A5E4; }

#tqi-card {
	position:fixed;
	z-index:99999;
	width:320px;
	background:#fff;
	border-radius:16px;
	box-shadow:0 8px 40px rgba(0,0,0,.18), 0 0 0 1px rgba(0,0,0,.06);
	overflow:hidden;
	font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Roboto,sans-serif;
	pointer-events:auto;
	opacity:0;
	transform:translateY(6px);
	transition:opacity .18s ease, transform .18s ease;
}
/* Invisible hover bridge sits ABOVE the card (as a separate fixed element,
   positioned in JS) to span the gap between the link and the card so the
   cursor never crosses an un-hovered zone. See #tqi-bridge below. */
#tqi-bridge {
	position:fixed;
	z-index:99998;
	pointer-events:auto;
	background:transparent;
	display:none;
}
#tqi-bridge.visible { display:block; }
#tqi-card.visible {
	opacity:1;
	transform:translateY(0);
}
#tqi-card-banner {
	height:5px;
	background:linear-gradient(90deg,#0AA0AB,#06b6d4);
}
#tqi-card-body {
	padding:16px;
}
#tqi-card-top {
	display:flex;
	align-items:center;
	gap:12px;
	margin-bottom:12px;
}
#tqi-card-logo {
	width:48px; height:48px; min-width:48px;
	border-radius:12px;
	background:#f8fafc;
	border:1px solid #e2e8f0;
	display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center;
	overflow:hidden;
	font-size:18px; font-weight:800; color:#0AA0AB;
	flex-shrink:0;
}
#tqi-card-logo img {
	max-width:100%; max-height:100%; object-fit:contain;
}
#tqi-card-name {
	font-weight:700;
	font-size:17px;
	color:#0f172a;
	line-height:1.25;
	margin-bottom:7px;
}
#tqi-card-type {
	display:inline-flex;
	align-items:center;
	gap:5px;
	font-size:12px;
	font-weight:600;
	color:#64748b;
	background:#f1f5f9;
	border:1px solid #e2e8f0;
	padding:3px 11px;
	border-radius:100px;
	line-height:1.4;
}
#tqi-card-desc {
	font-size:13px;
	color:#475569;
	line-height:1.6;
	margin-bottom:14px;
	display:-webkit-box;
	-webkit-line-clamp:5;
	-webkit-box-orient:vertical;
	overflow:hidden;
}
#tqi-card-desc.loading {
	color:#cbd5e1;
}
#tqi-card-links {
	display:flex;
	flex-direction:column;
	gap:8px;
}
.tqi-card-btn {
	display:flex;
	align-items:center;
	justify-content:space-between;
	gap:6px;
	text-decoration:none !important;
	font-size:14px;
	font-weight:600;
	padding:13px 18px;
	border-radius:12px;
	transition:background .15s, color .15s;
	cursor:pointer;
	width:100%;
	box-sizing:border-box;
}
.tqi-card-btn-primary {
	background:#0AA0AB;
	color:#fff !important;
}
.tqi-card-btn-primary:hover { background:#0891b2; }
.tqi-card-btn-secondary {
	background:#f1f5f9;
	color:#334155 !important;
}
.tqi-card-btn-secondary:hover { background:#e2e8f0; }
.tqi-card-btn svg { flex-shrink:0; }

/* ── Location row (renders only when data present) ── */
#tqi-card-location {
	display:flex;
	align-items:center;
	gap:6px;
	font-size:12px;
	color:#64748b;
	margin:-4px 0 12px;
}
#tqi-card-location .tqi-card-region { color:#94a3b8; }

/* ── FTEs badge (renders only when data present) ── */
#tqi-card-fte {
	display:flex;
	align-items:center;
	justify-content:space-between;
	margin:0 0 14px;
	padding-top:12px;
	border-top:1px solid #f1f5f9;
}
#tqi-card-fte .tqi-fte-label {
	font-size:11px;
	color:#94a3b8;
	font-weight:500;
	text-transform:uppercase;
	letter-spacing:.06em;
}
#tqi-card-fte .tqi-fte-value {
	font-size:12px;
	font-weight:600;
	color:#0AA0AB;
	background:rgba(10,160,171,.07);
	border:1px solid rgba(10,160,171,.2);
	padding:2px 10px;
	border-radius:100px;
}

/* ── TQI entity link styling (editor-inserted links only) ──
   Scoped to a.tqi-company-link so it NEVER affects legacy links,
   which are intentionally left unclassed to preserve page layout. */
a.tqi-company-link {
	color:inherit;
	text-decoration:underline;
	text-decoration-color:rgba(10,160,171,.45);
	text-underline-offset:3px;
	background-color:rgba(10,160,171,.09);
	border-radius:2px;
	transition:text-decoration-color .2s, color .2s, background-color .2s;
	cursor:pointer;
}
a.tqi-company-link:hover {
	color:#0AA0AB;
	text-decoration-color:#0AA0AB;
	background-color:rgba(10,160,171,.12);
}

				<div class="elementor-element elementor-element-55b9a366 elementor-widget elementor-widget-theme-post-content" data-id="55b9a366" data-element_type="widget" data-e-type="widget" data-widget_type="theme-post-content.default">
				<div class="elementor-widget-container">
					
<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The development of quantum computing companies over the last decade continues to accelerate, driven by the ambition to develop quantum processors and the tools ,  both hardware and software ,  that support them. </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">This article presents a snapshot of the evolving quantum computing ecosystem, as it stands today and heading into 2026.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">If you’re seeking deeper insights into these quantum companies ,  regularly updated and enhanced by our analyst team ,  you may want to explore our <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/data/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Quantum Intelligence Platform</a><strong>.</strong></p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Got an update or a suggestion? We welcome your input ,  feel free to reach out to us at the contact info page of <a href="https://resonance.holdings/#contact" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Resonance’s website</a>. </p>



<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Quantum Computing’s Role Across Industries</h2>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Quantum computing is steadily gaining ground in sectors like healthcare, energy, and finance, offering new ways to approach previously intractable computational problems. Unlike other complex technologies such as artificial intelligence (AI) or virtual reality (VR), quantum computing often remains a mystery to the broader public, understood in depth only by experts in the field.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Quantum computing is expected to perform tasks that are currently out of reach for classical systems ,  powered by purpose-built hardware and software stacks. Companies across the space are working to make this powerful technology more accessible and usable.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">As technological breakthroughs continue to unfold, the quantum landscape is expanding rapidly. Here, we explore how the ecosystem of quantum computing companies has taken shape over the last two decades.</p>



<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Where Does Our Data Come From?</h2>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">While our list highlights over seventy leading quantum players, it represents only a snapshot of the broader quantum landscape we track through our <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/data/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Quantum Data Intelligence Platform</a> – the world’s leading market intelligence source dedicated to quantum technologies. </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The platform aggregates and curates comprehensive data from across the global quantum ecosystem, including companies, investors, academic groups, and government initiatives, applying a custom taxonomy and metadata system designed to segment and classify the sector with precision. It combines AI-enhanced big data collection with expert analyst insights, ensuring that funding flows, strategic partnerships, and quantum technology developments are consistently updated, contextualized, and made actionable. By drawing on this foundation, our coverage not only spotlights the innovators shaping the quantum market today but also provides readers with a reliable, data-backed view of how the industry is advancing toward commercial quantum advantage.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Due to space constraints, this list of quantum computing companies is not exhaustive, and some quantum communications or cybersecurity firms are included only where they overlap with key hardware or software developments. Our classification reflects the dual nature of the quantum industry: a race led by incumbents with deep pockets and infrastructure, and a wave of nimble startups carving out novel approaches to quantum engineering, error correction, simulation, and more.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">This company list is current as of winter <strong>202</strong>6.</p>



<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Top Quantum Computing Companies in 2026</h2>



<h3 class="wp-block-heading">Leading Big Tech & Public Companies in Quantum (in Alphabetical Order)</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Several major U.S. corporations have taken center stage in quantum computing. IBM, with its century-long track record of tech innovation, leads this group. Meanwhile, Google, Microsoft, and AWS ,  though relatively younger ,  have each invested significantly in quantum R&D.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Other global players included in this section, which have gone public via SPAC, or Special Purpose Acquisition Company,<strong> </strong>also feature in our review, recognize the transformative potential of quantum computing across industries. These companies are ramping up their efforts to stay at the forefront as the sector evolves beyond the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era, toward fault-tolerant systems and, ultimately, quantum advantage ,  the tipping point where quantum machines usefully outperform their classical counterparts.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>1. Amazon Braket (Amazon)</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-full"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="264" height="147" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_GGswBYo6oLhNpjtFmhQYEg.jpg" alt="Amazon logo" class="wp-image-2359594"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://aws.amazon.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Amazon Braket</a>, launched in 2019 by Amazon Web Services (AWS), is a fully managed quantum computing service that provides researchers, developers, and enterprises with unified, on-demand access to a broad array of quantum hardware technologies and simulation tools. Designed to lower the barriers to entry in quantum computing, Braket enables users to design, test, and run quantum algorithms across multiple device types without needing to manage infrastructure.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Through a pay-as-you-go model and deep integration with the AWS ecosystem, users can quickly launch Jupyter notebooks, access pre-built quantum algorithms, and explore powerful simulators before deploying on actual quantum processors. Braket supports a diverse set of hardware from leading providers, including superconducting systems from Rigetti Computing, Oxford Quantum Circuits, neutral atom from QuEra, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/02/21/ionq-develops-assembly-chamber-for-miniaturized-room-temperature-ion-trap-vacuum-packages/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">ion-trap systems from IonQ</a>, quantum <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/03/13/d-wave-deep-dive-a-look-at-the-quantum-advantage-findings-and-the-questions-that-remain/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">annealers from D-Wave</a>, and <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/06/05/xanadu-demonstrates-scalable-building-block-for-photonic-quantum-computers/">photonic devices from Xanadu</a>. Additional ecosystem partners include Toshiba and IQM.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The platform includes a range of simulation tools tailored to different quantum circuit types and levels of complexity, such as SV1 (a state vector simulator), TN1 (a tensor network simulator), DM1 (a noise-aware density matrix simulator), and a free local simulator. This simulator suite allows users to validate and optimize their algorithms before committing resources to physical quantum hardware.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In 2023, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2023/12/06/aws-announces-three-programs-designed-to-make-quantum-research-easier-learning-more-accessible/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">AWS introduced Braket Direct</a>, a reservation-based program that grants exclusive access to high-performance quantum devices, including IonQ’s 30-qubit Forte system. It also launched the Amazon Braket Digital Learning Plan, a free educational program designed to help developers build foundational skills in quantum computing and earn digital credentials. Amazon continues to support internal R&D for quantum hardware through tools such as Palace, a scalable parallel codebase used for large-scale 3D electromagnetics simulations essential to hardware design.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Braket’s capabilities have continued to expand. In 2024, it integrated <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/08/28/amazon-braket-cloud-computing-platform-announces-rigetti-ankaa-2-superconducting-quantum-processor-for-continuous-access/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Rigetti’s 84-qubit Ankaa-2 processor</a>, the company’s most advanced superconducting quantum system, and introduced the Quantum Embark Program. This initiative offers a structured, modular approach for enterprises to explore, test, and prepare for quantum adoption, supporting everything from use-case discovery to algorithm development.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In February 2025, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/02/27/aws-unveils-ocelot-its-first-quantum-computing-chip/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">AWS unveiled Ocelot</a>, its first proprietary quantum chip developed at the AWS Center for Quantum Computing at Caltech. Ocelot is built with cat qubits that suppress environmental noise and reduce error-correction overhead, using an architecture of 14 physical qubits including buffers and detection circuits. Operating with minimal power requirements, Ocelot represents AWS’s growing investment in hardware innovation.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Amazon Braket is tightly integrated with AWS cloud infrastructure, offering scalable resources and <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2022/05/19/aws-braket-is-helping-to-make-the-quantum-industry-more-transparent/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">developer-friendly environments</a> that make it accessible to users at any level of quantum expertise. With continued advances in hardware, simulation, education, and enterprise programs, Braket is positioning itself as a central hub for scalable, hybrid quantum-classical research and development across academia, industry, and government.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/amazon-braket/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Amazon Braket</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>2. D-Wave Systems</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="68" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_Qb_wy4aCIo6DeDtS1dEJLg-300x68.png" alt="D-wave logo" class="wp-image-2359608" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_Qb_wy4aCIo6DeDtS1dEJLg-300x68.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_Qb_wy4aCIo6DeDtS1dEJLg.png 471w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.dwavequantum.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">D-Wave Quantum Inc.</a> is a publicly traded, full-stack quantum computing company founded in 1999, known for pioneering quantum annealing and now advancing into gate-based superconducting quantum computing. With 100+ employees and over 100 enterprise and institutional partners, D-Wave offers both quantum hardware and cloud-based software tools to solve complex optimization problems across industries like finance, life sciences, materials science, and AI/ML.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">D-Wave’s LEAP platform provides real-time cloud access to its quantum systems, software development kits (Ocean), and hybrid solvers. <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/05/20/d-wave-announces-general-availability-of-advantage2-quantum-computer/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Their Advantage2 processor</a>, launched in 2024, includes over 4,400+ qubits with improved coherence, a new Zephyr topology, and 20-way qubit connectivity, significantly enhancing problem-solving performance. The processor is expected to deliver up to 25,000× speedups for some materials science tasks and 5× higher precision for high-complexity applications.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">D-Wave is also addressing quantum error mitigation and recently expanded LEAP to support generative AI, RBM-based training, and GPU integration. Collaborations, such as with <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/03/31/d-wave-and-japan-tobacco-validate-quantum-and-ai-workflow-towards-generative-drug-discovery/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Japan Tobacco’s pharma unit</a>, have demonstrated quantum’s value in drug discovery. Meanwhile, new deployments like a secure<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/04/24/d-wave-davidson-technologies-near-installation-completion-of-alabamas-first-on-site-annealing-quantum-computer/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> U.S.-based system with Davidson Technologies</a> underline D-Wave’s move into sensitive government and defense applications.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Their January 2025<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/01/22/d-wave-announces-new-leap-quantum-launchpad-program-to-support-quantum-computing-applications/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> Leap Quantum LaunchPad program</a> offers startups and researchers a three-month free trial to access their 5,000+ qubit systems, development tools, and expert support ,  further broadening adoption and application of quantum technology.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/d-wave/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on D-Wave Systems</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>3. IBM</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="120" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_G7FLLce-JdtMqv4Z0w1d0g-300x120.png" alt="IBM logo" class="wp-image-2359590" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_G7FLLce-JdtMqv4Z0w1d0g-300x120.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_G7FLLce-JdtMqv4Z0w1d0g.png 355w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"></p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Founded in 1911 in Endicott, New York by Charles Ranlett Flint,<a href="https://www.ibm.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow"> IBM</a> has grown into one of the world’s most enduring and influential technology companies. In the field of quantum computing, IBM maintains a leading position through its robust hardware portfolio, open-source tooling, and comprehensive cloud services. Its quantum ecosystem includes the IBM Quantum Composer and the IBM Quantum Lab (<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2020/04/28/ibm-quantum-experience-announces-a-global-quantum-challenge/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">formerly IBM Quantum Experience</a>), both of which provide public and premium access to IBM’s quantum systems. These cloud-based platforms enable users to run algorithms, explore simulations, and engage with educational resources including tutorials and an interactive quantum computing textbook.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">IBM’s quantum hardware is based on superconducting qubits and is supported by a suite of advanced software tools including Qiskit and OpenQASM. The company operates the world’s largest fleet of quantum systems for real-world applications through its <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/09/26/ibm-expands-quantum-data-center-in-poughkeepsie-new-york-to-advance-algorithm-discovery-globally/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Quantum Computation Center in New York</a>. Notable processors include Osprey with 433 qubits, Condor with 1,121 qubits, and the high-fidelity Heron series. These are deployed via IBM Quantum System One and the modular System Two architecture, accessible through cloud offerings like IBM Quantum Safe, Qiskit Runtime, and Qiskit Serverless.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In 2024, IBM expanded its infrastructure and software capabilities by <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/10/01/ibm-opens-its-first-quantum-data-center-in-europe-featuring-16x-more-performant-heron-based-systems/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">opening its first European Quantum Data Center</a> in Germany and <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/10/11/ibm-releases-qiskit-code-assistant-to-streamline-quantum-coding-with-ai-tools/">launching new tools such as Qiskit Code Assistant</a> and Guardium Quantum Safe. The company also introduced fractional gate operations to reduce quantum circuit depth and improve algorithm performance. <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/10/12/ibm-quantum-roadmap-guide-scaling-and-expanding-the-usefulness-of-quantum-computing/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">IBM’s long-term roadmap</a> stretches to 2033, with milestones targeting scalable, fault-tolerant quantum systems enabled through modular and hybrid system designs. The company continues to build strategic partnerships, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/11/22/ibm-and-pasqal-unite-to-build-a-quantum-centric-supercomputing-framework-with-qiskit/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">including with firms like Pasqal</a>, to foster interoperability and advance quantum-centric supercomputing.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">IBM’s quantum vision encompasses not just hardware, but a full-stack, cloud-accessible, and developer-focused platform designed to bring quantum advantage into real-world domains like finance, materials science, and cryptography. With a legacy of innovation and a forward-looking strategy, IBM remains a cornerstone of the global quantum ecosystem.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/ibm/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on IBM</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>4. IonQ</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="105" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_wJCSgl5zX8D_8YTOYOXpMg-300x105.png" alt="Ionq logo" class="wp-image-2359618" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_wJCSgl5zX8D_8YTOYOXpMg-300x105.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_wJCSgl5zX8D_8YTOYOXpMg.png 380w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://ionq.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">IonQ</a> is a public quantum computing company founded in 2015 (with a heritage spanning decades), specializing in trapped ion quantum computers and offers a full-stack quantum platform accessible via major cloud services like Amazon Braket and its own IonQ Quantum Cloud. Its systems are designed for high-fidelity, rack-mounted deployment, enabling seamless integration into modern data centers.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company has introduced multiple quantum systems <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2023/05/19/ionq-aria-now-available-on-amazon-braket/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">including IonQ Aria</a>, Forte, and the enterprise-grade Forte Enterprise, which reached 36 algorithmic qubits (AQ36) as of December 2024. The Forte system incorporates acousto-optic deflector (AOD) technology, enabling precise laser targeting to individual ions ,  critical for reducing noise and improving scalability. The platform supports use cases in optimization, quantum machine learning, finance, AI, and chemistry, and is actively being deployed in research hubs across the U.S. and Europe, including <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2023/06/22/ionq-and-quantumbasel-partner-to-deploy-of-two-generations-of-ionq-quantum-systems-in-europe/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">partnerships with QuantumBasel</a> in Switzerland and the University of Maryland’s QLab.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">IonQ is pioneering quantum networking through its photonic interconnects ,  enabling entanglement between multiple QPUs ,  and recently <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/11/06/ionq-announces-acquisition-of-qubitekk/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">acquired Qubitekk</a>, a leader in quantum communications. Its novel Clifford Noise Reduction (CliNR) technique has drastically reduced the overhead for quantum error correction, a significant step toward scalable, near-term quantum applications.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company has secured major enterprise and government partnerships, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2021/11/24/dell-and-ionq-partner-to-test-classical-quantum-hybrid-system/">including Dell</a>, Airbus, the Naval Research Lab, and DESY, while operating out of multiple data centers in College Park (MD), Seattle, and Basel. In March 2025, this quantum company <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/03/11/ionq-raises-360m-through-atm-program-terminates-offering-after-meeting-capital-needs/">raised $360 million through an ATM equity offering</a>, boosting its cash reserves to over $700 million and ensuring funding for both its computing and networking roadmaps. IonQ subsequently <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/07/07/ionq-announces-pricing-of-1-0-billion-equity-offering/">raised an additional $1 billion</a>, bringing its cash position to roughly $1.6 billion, giving it one of the strongest balance sheets in the quantum sector.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">IonQ has aggressively expanded through strategic acquisitions in 2024–2025. It <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/01/06/ionq-expands-quantum-networking-abilities-with-qubitekk-acquisition/">completed the acquisition of Qubitekk</a> in January 2025, strengthening its quantum-networking capabilities and patent base. In February, it <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/05/06/ionq-completes-acquisition-of-id-quantique-cementing-leadership-in-quantum-networking-and-secure-communications/">secured control over Geneva-based ID Quantique</a>, bolstering its quantum-safe cryptography and detection portfolio. By mid-year, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/07/15/ionq-completes-acquisition-of-capella-space/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">IonQ closed on Capella Space (July 2025)</a>, adding Earth-observation satellite assets to enable space-based QKD networks. Earlier acquisitions include Lightsynq, enhancing its quantum memory roadmap, and most recently, the June 2025 announcement to <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/06/09/ionq-acquires-uk-based-oxford-ionics-for-1-075-billion/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">acquire Oxford Ionics for approximately $1.075 billion</a> in stock and cash, bringing ion-trap-on-a-chip technology and a clear path toward fault-tolerant systems with over two million physical qubits by 2030.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/ionq/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on IonQ</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>5. Google Quantum AI</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="79" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_Jh0PqTcoHOd-r6jq6BLZ6A-e1757717596221-300x79.png" alt="Google Quantum AI logo" class="wp-image-2359592" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_Jh0PqTcoHOd-r6jq6BLZ6A-e1757717596221-300x79.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_Jh0PqTcoHOd-r6jq6BLZ6A-e1757717596221.png 309w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://quantumai.google/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Google Quantum AI</a> is working to  develop practical and transformative quantum computing technologies, combining advances in superconducting hardware with cutting-edge quantum software. Founded in 2013 as a collaboration between Google, NASA, and the Universities Space Research Association, the Quantum Artificial Intelligence Lab quickly became a hub for innovation. In 2019, it achieved global recognition by claiming quantum supremacy, demonstrating that its <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2022/07/14/google-sycamore/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Sycamore processor</a> could complete a task infeasible for classical computers within a reasonable timeframe ,  a milestone that marked the beginning of a new era in quantum experimentation.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The program is guided by Engineering Director Hartmut Neven and is structured around a clear, step-by-step roadmap toward fault-tolerant quantum computing. Google’s long-term goal is to develop large-scale, error-corrected systems capable of solving real-world problems in fields like logistics, artificial intelligence, quantum chemistry, and foundational computer science. Its quantum hardware is <a href="https://qmunity.thequantuminsider.com/2024/04/29/quantum-supremacy-with-sycamore/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">based on the Sycamore architecture</a>, with recent progress in quantum error correction enabling logical qubits to outperform physical ones. As of 2024, Google’s 101-qubit device achieved a<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/08/27/breaking-the-surface-google-demonstrates-error-correction-below-surface-code-threshold/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> 0.143% error-per-cycle rate</a>, improving logical qubit lifetimes by a factor of 2.4. The company also introduced<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/11/20/ai-power-for-quantum-errors-google-develops-alphaqubit-to-identify-correct-quantum-errors/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> tools like AlphaQubit</a>, an AI-powered decoder that accelerates error correction, and <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/10/23/luci-in-the-surface-codes-with-drop-outs-google-quantum-ai-researchers-report-framework-could-help-reduce-errors/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">LUCI</a>, a dynamic framework for fault-tolerant quantum circuit adaptation that reduces logical error rates by a factor of 36.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">On the software side, Google has created a rich ecosystem for quantum algorithm development. Cirq, its open-source Python framework, supports near-term quantum algorithm design on both simulators and real devices. TensorFlow Quantum bridges classical machine learning with quantum computation, while OpenFermion provides tools for simulating fermionic systems, particularly in quantum chemistry. Google Quantum AI continues to explore the fusion of quantum computing with artificial intelligence, enabling hybrid classical-quantum models to prototype machine learning solutions.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The team is also advancing hardware scalability through investments in cryogenic infrastructure and modular hybrid architectures. In <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/11/19/nvidia-accelerates-google-quantum-ai-processor-design-with-simulation-of-quantum-device-physics/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">collaboration with NVIDIA</a>, Google simulates 40-qubit systems using more than 1,000 H100 GPUs, reinforcing its commitment to integrated classical-quantum processing. In 2024, the company further deepened its reach into the quantum ecosystem by<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/10/15/quera-computing-announces-investment-from-google-quantum-ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> investing in QuEra Computing</a>, aiming to enhance joint capabilities in quantum error correction.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Google Quantum AI’s new 105-qubit Willow chip marks a major milestone in the company’s quantum computing journey, showcasing enhanced computational power, scalable error correction, and a clear path toward commercially viable systems. In benchmark testing, Willow completed in minutes a calculation that would take the fastest classical supercomputer an astronomical timeframe, highlighting the exponential advantage quantum computing holds over classical methods. Most importantly, by demonstrating that increasing qubit numbers can actually reduce errors, Willow validates a fundamental approach to quantum error correction and paves the way toward large-scale, fault-tolerant quantum machines capable of addressing real-world challenges.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">With a combination of groundbreaking research, open-source tool development, and strategic partnerships, Google Quantum AI remains one of the most influential players in the global quantum computing race. Its vision is not only to build fault-tolerant quantum systems but to unlock their potential across a wide spectrum of applications and industries.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/google-quantum-ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Google Quantum AI</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>6. Microsoft</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-full"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="299" height="95" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_5bWPwl1mE4M61jzi8NeuJQ-e1757717629173.png" alt="Microsoft logo" class="wp-image-2359593"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.microsoft.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Microsoft </a>has positioned itself as a key player in the global quantum computing race, driven by a long-term vision of developing scalable, fault-tolerant systems built on topological qubit architecture. Its quantum program is <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/01/03/how-microsoft-and-partners-are-shaping-the-future-of-quantum-computing/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">anchored by Azure Quantum</a>, a cloud-based, hardware-agnostic platform that brings together software tools, third-party quantum hardware, and cloud-native development environments. This open ecosystem enables developers, researchers, and enterprises to build quantum algorithms today that are compatible with future quantum hardware ,  bridging near-term experimentation with long-term strategic capability.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Microsoft’s entry into quantum computing followed the efforts of its Quantum Architectures and Computation (QuArC) group, based in Redmond and <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/04/12/microsofts-krysta-svore-explores-the-present-status-of-qc-its-future-implications-on-hansel-minutes-podcast/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">led by Krysta Svore</a>, which laid the early groundwork for quantum algorithm development and quantum circuit theory. In 2017, Microsoft launched Q#, a domain-specific programming language for quantum computing, along with its Quantum Development Kit (QDK), offering compilers, emulators, and integration with Visual Studio and Python. The QDK was open-sourced in 2019 to encourage transparency and community participation after a setback involving a retracted scientific paper on<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/02/19/microsofts-majorana-topological-chip-an-advance-17-years-in-the-making/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> Majorana particles</a>, a foundational element in Microsoft’s topological qubit approach.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In recent years, Microsoft has doubled down on its topological strategy. In July 2025, it published findings on its Majorana 1 chip, a new quantum prototype built using novel “topoconductor” materials to stabilize Majorana zero modes. While initial benchmarking met “Milestone 1” on Microsoft’s roadmap, key experimental validations ,  such as demonstrating anti-commutation between Pauli measurements ,  remain incomplete, meaning a true topological qubit has not yet been realized. Despite these hurdles, the company views topological qubits as a pathway to massively scalable systems with inherently low error rates, capable of reaching a million-qubit regime.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Azure Quantum continues to evolve, integrating quantum hardware from partners like Quantinuum and Atom Computing. In 2025, Microsoft collaborated with Quantinuum to achieve high-fidelity entanglement of 12 logical qubits and is co-developing a 24-logical-qubit commercial system with Atom Computing. The company has also introduced AI-powered quantum chemistry tools ,  Generative Chemistry and Accelerated DFT ,  and added post-quantum cryptography capabilities to SymCrypt, its core cryptographic library used across Azure and Microsoft 365.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Software advancements have kept pace with hardware ambition. Microsoft has open-sourced its Azure Quantum Resource Estimator, allowing developers to simulate resource requirements for error-corrected quantum algorithms. Meanwhile, the Azure Quantum platform continues to serve as a unifying layer for both hardware and software, with tools that facilitate the integration of quantum algorithms into existing classical workflows.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Although Microsoft closed its <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/07/16/winding-down-under-microsoft-shuts-down-quantum-research-in-australia-relocates-to-us/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">quantum research facility in Sydney</a> in 2024 to consolidate operations in the United States, the company remains globally engaged, collaborating with academic, industrial, and governmental partners. Under the leadership of Julie Love, Partner and Product Leader for Quantum Computing, Microsoft’s quantum strategy reflects both patience and persistence, focusing on high-risk, high-reward innovation to ultimately achieve practical quantum advantage through topological fault-tolerant architectures.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/microsoft/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Microsoft</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading">7. NVIDIA</h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image"><img decoding="async" src="https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1600/1*2xQz_kHRrtsDOCJTt2oHmg.png" alt="" width="792" height="536"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">NVIDIA is a core enabler of hybrid quantum–classical computing and does not build its own quantum processing units (QPUs). Instead, the company supplies the acceleration stack — GPUs, software, and reference architectures — that lets developers design, simulate, and orchestrate quantum workloads alongside classical HPC and AI.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">NVIDIA’s quantum software foundation is CUDA-Q (formerly QODA), an open, qubit-agnostic programming model for writing hybrid CPU/GPU/QPU workflows. CUDA-Q targets external quantum hardware through provider plug-ins while also driving high-performance simulation on NVIDIA GPUs. Complementing this, cuQuantum delivers optimized libraries for state-vector and tensor-network simulation, enabling researchers to validate algorithms, study error mechanisms, and prototype error-correction strategies at scales impractical on current QPUs.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">For enterprise and cloud deployment, NVIDIA Quantum Cloud packages these capabilities as managed microservices, integrating with major public clouds so teams can spin up large simulations without building bespoke HPC environments. On the systems side, DGX Quantum — co-developed with Quantum Machines — provides a reference architecture that tightly couples GPU nodes to QPU control hardware, reducing latency and simplifying real-time feedback between classical accelerators and external quantum devices.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">NVIDIA’s ecosystem is intentionally QPU-agnostic. The company partners with leading hardware makers and labs across trapped-ion, superconducting, neutral-atom, photonic, and annealing modalities, positioning its stack as the common layer for development, scheduling, calibration assistance, and large-scale simulation. High-visibility collaborations have used fleets of NVIDIA GPUs to emulate complex device physics and mid-scale circuits, stress-testing error-correction schemes and control protocols before committing to scarce QPU time.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The result is a pragmatic role: accelerate near-term research and industrial experimentation, standardize hybrid programming patterns, and compress iteration cycles — while leaving QPU design and manufacturing to specialized vendors. With CUDA-Q, cuQuantum, Quantum Cloud, and DGX Quantum, NVIDIA has become a default backbone for quantum R&D across academia, industry, and government — without competing as a QPU builder.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/09/13/how-is-nvidia-betting-on-quantum-a-look-at-nventures-quantum-investment-playbook-from-skepticism-to-strategic-stakes/">recent news on NVIDIA</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>8. Quantum Computing Inc.</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="39" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/download-2023-12-11T180725.435-300x39.png" alt="Quantum Computing inc logo" class="wp-image-2359643" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/download-2023-12-11T180725.435-300x39.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/download-2023-12-11T180725.435.png 600w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://quantumcomputinginc.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Quantum Computing Inc. </a>(QCI), founded in 2018, is a public quantum company that delivers full-stack quantum solutions, with a specialization in photonic quantum computing. With a team of 11–50 employees, QCI offers platforms tailored to complex optimization and security challenges across sectors such as finance and software.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company significantly expanded its capabilities following the 2022 <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2022/02/24/qci-and-qphoton-announce-exclusive-marketing-agreement-for-photonic-quantum-processing-technologies/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">acquisition of QPhoton</a>, which enhanced its quantum photonics expertise across both computing and sensing. Its flagship product, the Entropy Quantum Computing system (EQC), utilizes photonic architectures. In 2023, QCI broadened its commercial offering by <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/05/28/study-quantum-random-number-generator-almost-1000-times-faster-than-other-approaches/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">launching a Quantum Random Number Generator (QRNG)</a>, capable of producing truly random number sequences for secure applications. Initially offered via cloud subscription, a physical, handheld QRNG device followed later that year.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In March 2024, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/09/30/quantum-computing-inc-extends-partnership-with-los-alamos-national-lab-to-advance-dirac-3-quantum-optimization-machine/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">QCI introduced Dirac-3</a>, a photonics-based quantum optimization platform that uses nonlinear quantum optics to encode and solve integer optimization problems. Dirac-3 operates at room temperature, consumes only 100 watts of power, and supports up to 964 qudits with 200 discrete modes each ,  surpassing conventional qubit-based architectures in certain applications. Its 6U rack-mountable form factor is designed for on-premise enterprise use without special infrastructure.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">QCI has also ventured into photonic chip manufacturing. In November 2024, it received its first commercial order for its thin film lithium niobate (TFLN) photonic chip foundry from a major research and technology institute in Asia. This order leverages QCI’s proprietary techniques in etching, processing, and wafer-scale photonic characterization.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">QCI maintains a small but growing network of partnerships and investors, including a collaboration with millionways to integrate AI sensing algorithms with QCI’s photonic quantum computing capabilities. Through these developments, QCI continues to position itself as an innovator in compact, low-energy photonic quantum systems and related quantum hardware technologies.</p>

<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>9. Rigetti Computing</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="158" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_qa5qqPgivoeAj_1KMKNnDg-300x158.png" alt="Rigetti Logo" class="wp-image-2359654" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_qa5qqPgivoeAj_1KMKNnDg-300x158.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_qa5qqPgivoeAj_1KMKNnDg.png 310w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Rigetti Computing logo</figcaption></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.rigetti.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Rigetti Computing</a> is a public quantum computing company founded in 2013 and headquartered in Berkeley, California. It specializes in superconducting qubit-based quantum processors and provides both quantum hardware and software through its Quantum Cloud Services (QCS) platform. Rigetti’s full-stack offerings include quantum-classical infrastructure for ultra-low latency, multi-cloud access, and a proprietary software development kit (Forest SDK), which includes pyQuil, the Quil compiler (QuilC), and a Quantum Virtual Machine (QVM).</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Rigetti operates some of the most advanced superconducting quantum processors on the market. It was one of the first to deliver quantum computing over the cloud in 2017 and has since made its hardware available on platforms like Microsoft Azure and Amazon Braket. Its current systems include the 84-qubit Ankaa-2, based on Rigetti’s fourth-generation chip architecture featuring tunable couplers and a square lattice for high-fidelity 2-qubit operations, achieving a 98% median fidelity. Future systems<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/12/23/rigetti-computing-reports-84-qubit-ankaa-3-system-achieves-99-5-median-two-qubit-gate-fidelity-milestone/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> include the Ankaa-3</a>, aiming for 99+% fidelity, and a modular system with over 100 qubits planned by the end of 2025.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2023/12/09/rigetti-launches-novera-qpu-the-companys-first-commercially-available-qpu/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Rigetti’s Novera QPU</a>, launched in Q4 2023 marked the company’s first commercially available QPU, combining both a 9-qubit chip and a simpler 5-qubit chip for advanced benchmarking and development. The Novera system is integrated with comprehensive cooling, shielding, and signal conditioning systems, reflecting Rigetti’s commitment to robust and scalable system engineering.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/10/31/rigetti-and-riverlane-achieve-real-time-quantum-error-correction-on-84-qubit-system/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">In collaboration with Riverlane</a>, Rigetti achieved a breakthrough in real-time, low-latency quantum error correction in October 2024. This demonstration on Ankaa-2 showed decoding times under one microsecond, a critical threshold for hybrid quantum-classical operations and a major step toward fault-tolerant quantum computing.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company has also developed a novel chip fabrication method called Alternating-Bias Assisted Annealing (ABAA), which improves qubit coherence and two-qubit gate performance. This method is being applied to the Novera and upcoming Ankaa-3 systems.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Rigetti’s client base includes 14 enterprise users and over 100 organizations, government partners, and academic institutions. Its strategic collaborations include Keysight Technologies, NVIDIA, Bluefors, and Microsoft Azure. In April 2025, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/04/30/rigetti-finalizes-35-million-investment-as-quanta-partnership-moves-forward/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Quanta Computer invested $35 million into Rigetti</a>, acquiring shares at approximately $11.59 each, strengthening their long-term partnership.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Looking ahead, Rigetti plans to release a 36-qubit modular system in mid-2025, with a 100+ qubit system expected by the end of the year. These developments position Rigetti as one of the key players in the race toward scalable, high-performance quantum computing.</p>

<h3 class="wp-block-heading">Other Quantum Computing Companies & Scale-ups</h3>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><em>-in alphabetical order-</em></p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">While Big Tech firms have set the pace in quantum computing through massive R&D investments, the startup ecosystem has become an equally vital driver of innovation. These younger companies are not only experimenting with diverse qubit modalities but also building the infrastructure, software and tooling needed to commercialize quantum technologies. From spinouts of academic research groups to fast-growing software platforms, startups are pushing the boundaries across hardware, cryptography, and simulation. What follows is a curated list of active quantum firms helping shape the next phase of this industry ,  each contributing uniquely to the global quantum landscape.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>10. 1QBit</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="95" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_A3I_SVqjpPAOoacJmJFDYA-300x95.png" alt="1QBit Logo" class="wp-image-2359678" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_A3I_SVqjpPAOoacJmJFDYA-300x95.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_A3I_SVqjpPAOoacJmJFDYA.png 400w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://1qbit.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">1QBit</a> is a private quantum software company founded in 2012 that specializes in developing hardware-agnostic tools for quantum computing. The company focuses on creating general-purpose algorithms that can run across different quantum computing hardware platforms. Its technology is designed to support both near-term, noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) systems and future, fault-tolerant quantum computers.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company was co-founded by Andrew Fursman, Landon Downs and Hamidreza Han, who initially approached D-Wave in the 2000s with the idea of building a pure software layer for quantum annealers. From these early roots, 1QBit evolved into one of the leading players in <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2021/12/12/quco-1qbit-and-ntt-qc-ware-among-winners-of-bmw-group-quantum-computing-challenge/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">applied quantum software development</a>. Although originally focused on NISQ-era applications, the company has increasingly expanded its mission to deliver enabling software that supports the entire quantum technology stack.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">1QBit has also established itself as an incubator for new ventures at the intersection of quantum computing and deep technology. It has spun off two significant companies: Synthesise, which delivers medical technology solutions and operates distributed radiology, imaging, and laboratory clinics; and Good Chemistry, which uses quantum computing to solve complex problems in computational chemistry. These spinouts highlight 1QBit’s role not just as a software provider, but as a catalyst for broader innovation in the quantum ecosystem.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">With partnerships that include 11 enterprise users, 45 organizations, 5 research centers, and backing from 11 investors, 1QBit maintains a strong collaborative network. Its work has applications across multiple sectors, including life sciences, finance, logistics, and advanced materials, and its platform offers full-stack capabilities designed to bridge today’s classical systems with the emerging quantum landscape.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/1qbit/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> news on 1QBit</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>11. Alice & Bob </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-thumbnail"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="150" height="150" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_sRs2gUjZa9g-lr29kL83qQ-150x150.png" alt="Alice & Bob logo" class="wp-image-2359600" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_sRs2gUjZa9g-lr29kL83qQ-150x150.png 150w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_sRs2gUjZa9g-lr29kL83qQ.png 224w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 150px) 100vw, 150px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://alice-bob.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Alice & Bob</a> is a private quantum computing company founded in 2020 that is focused on building a fault-tolerant, error-corrected quantum computer using a novel approach based on superconducting circuits known as Cat Qubits. The company has established itself as a leader in hardware innovation for scalable quantum systems, developing self-correcting qubits that dramatically reduce the need for extensive error correction infrastructure.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Unlike traditional qubits, Cat Qubits are capable of autonomously stabilizing quantum information in two particular states, effectively suppressing bit-flip errors and extending coherence times. In March 2022, Alice & Bob announced a major breakthrough in quantum error correction by demonstrating macroscopic bit-flip times of up to two minutes, significantly improving upon previous records. This research validated their stabilization scheme as a powerful step toward truly fault-tolerant quantum computing. By May 2024, the company <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/05/15/alice-bobs-first-cat-qubit-quantum-chip-available-on-google-cloud-marketplace/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">launched its Boson 4 chip</a> ,  a single Cat Qubit processor featuring 11 photons ,  on the Google Cloud Marketplace. The Boson 4 achieved a bit-flip lifetime of over seven minutes for a single qubit, a remarkable leap in the performance of superconducting quantum systems.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company is not only advancing its hardware but also building critical infrastructure and tooling for the broader ecosystem. In November 2024,<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/11/20/alice-bob-launch-dynamiqs-quantum-simulation-library-powered-by-nvidia-accelerated-computing/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener"> Alice & Bob released Dynamiqs</a>, an open-source quantum simulation library that leverages NVIDIA’s accelerated computing to deliver simulations up to 60 times faster. This library, built using JAX and Diffrax, addresses key bottlenecks in simulating large, open, and time-dependent quantum systems. That same month, they <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/11/26/alice-bob-enable-companies-to-prepare-for-practical-quantum-computing-with-logical-qubit-emulator/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">launched Felis 1.0</a>, a logical qubit emulator built on Qiskit and integrated with Classiq. Felis is designed to help developers transition from NISQ devices to logical qubit-based systems, offering tools for algorithm optimization, hardware tuning, and a forthcoming cat qubit resource estimator.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Beyond R&D, Alice & Bob has expanded its offerings with a consulting group launched in September 2023, led by Dr. Linde Hansen. This strategic unit provides businesses with quantum adoption guidance, complementing a technical collaboration announced in November 2023 with Equinix, Inc., which enables secure access to Alice & Bob’s quantum technology via Equinix Metal and Equinix Fabric. The partnership is aimed at accelerating enterprise readiness for the quantum era.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Alice & Bob’s contributions to the quantum field have been supported by major institutions including ENS de Lyon, Mines Paris ,  PSL, and Inria. Together, they have advanced new error correction architectures based on low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes, potentially enabling the operation of 100 high-fidelity logical qubits using as few as 1,500 physical Cat Qubits. Their efforts have been recognized through a €16.5 million ($17.8 million) grant <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/12/04/from-cat-qubits-to-universal-quantum-computing-alice-bobs-2030-roadmap-delivers-high-fidelity-solutions/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">from the France 2030 initiative</a>, administered by Bpifrance, for a 36-month project to enhance the efficiency of their quantum stack and reduce manufacturing costs.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">With a growing network of six enterprise users, nine partner organizations, four research centers, and six investors, Alice & Bob continues to shape the frontier of fault-tolerant quantum computing. The company is a member of key consortia including Les Maisons du Quantique, QED-C, and Le Lab Quantique.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/alice-bob/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Alice & Bob</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>12. Alpine Quantum Technologies (AQT) </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-thumbnail"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="150" height="95" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_6d5GWcx7dLwudKLoAL6q7g-e1757717506149-150x95.jpg" alt="AQT Logo" class="wp-image-2359601"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.aqt.eu/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Alpine Quantum Technologies (AQT)</a> is a private company founded in 2018 that specializes in the development of trapped ion quantum computing systems. AQT is based in Europe and has emerged as one of the continent’s leading players <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2022/02/19/6-quantum-computing-companies-working-with-trapped-ions/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">in trapped ion hardware innovation</a>, offering a full-stack quantum solution accessible via the cloud.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">AQT’s <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/09/16/ion-trap-quantum-computer-ready-for-novel-research-and-development-at-the-lrz/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">ion-trap quantum devices</a> operate by precisely directing laser beams onto the position of one or more ions to manipulate their quantum states. This approach has enabled the company to achieve high-fidelity qubits and remarkable levels of entanglement. As of December 2022, AQT has built a 20-qubit register with next-neighbor coupling rates of approximately 0.6%, which can be suppressed to the 1e-7 level using advanced error mitigation techniques. These metrics meet the stringent requirements for fault-tolerant quantum computing, positioning AQT as a serious contender in the global race for scalable quantum systems.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company is committed to interoperability and supports a wide range of leading quantum development frameworks, including Qiskit, Cirq, PennyLane, and Pytket. This allows developers to run existing quantum code directly on AQT’s devices with minimal modification. Their systems have already been deployed as experimental platforms to explore quantum advantages in fields such as quantum chemistry, portfolio optimization, risk analysis, cryptographic security, and decryption.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">AQT has achieved a European record in quantum volume with a score of 128, underlining its technical maturity. In addition, AQT has developed the PINE system ,  a quantum computer that fits in a standard 19-inch rack and operates at room temperature, significantly lowering barriers to adoption. It also holds the world record for 24-qubit entanglement and has created the first universal gate set for logical qubits with integrated error correction.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company provides a comprehensive offering that includes cloud-based quantum computing access (<a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2021/04/20/the-quantum-daily-launches-quantum-computing-as-a-service-qcaas-project/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">QCaaS</a>), quantum simulation (both ideal and realistic ion trap models), and consulting services. It also supports classical compute resources (CPU and GPU) alongside its quantum systems. Access to AQT’s hardware is available via T-Systems International and directly through Alpine Quantum Technologies.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">AQT is a member of the European Quantum Industry Consortium (QuIC) and maintains active partnerships with three organizations and three research centers.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/alpine-quantum-technologies/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Alpine Quantum Technologies</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>13. Anyon Systems</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-full"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="52" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Anyon-Logo-300x52-1.webp" alt="Anyon logo" class="wp-image-2359602"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://anyonsys.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Anyon Systems</a>, founded in 2014, is a vertically integrated quantum computing company whose focus is in on-premise superconducting quantum computers. Based in Canada, the company develops the full stack ,  including quantum chips, cryogenics, control electronics, and software ,  in-house. Their flagship system, Qube™, is a modular, stand-alone quantum computer designed to integrate seamlessly into high-performance computing (HPC) centers. It features a closed-cycle cryogenic system for minimal maintenance and can scale with upgraded qubit chips.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Anyon Systems has delivered <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2020/12/16/anyon-systems-to-deliver-a-quantum-computer-to-the-canadian-department-of-national-defense/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">quantum computers to Canadian government entities</a> and is now supplying a full system to an HPC center. The company has also supported Google with quantum simulation efforts.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">To support quantum software development, Anyon has created Snowflake™, an open-source library for quantum circuit design, and Snowflurry™, a Julia-based tool tailored for HPC environments. Snowflurry™ is the recommended interface for running quantum workloads on Anyon’s processors and contributes to the broader open-source quantum ecosystem.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/anyon-systems/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Anyon Systems</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>14 Atlantic Quantum </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-full"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="297" height="170" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_-6txF2QlPGGj87yIJG-2JQ.png" alt="Atlantic Quantum Logo" class="wp-image-2359603"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://atlantic-quantum.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Atlantic Quantum</a> is a private quantum computing company founded in 2022 with the ambitious goal of building a fault-tolerant quantum computer that can scale to the million-qubit regime. Based on superconducting technology, Atlantic Quantum is pioneering a hardware approach that centers on enhancing qubit coherence ,  a critical factor in reducing quantum errors and enabling large-scale, practical quantum computation.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Unlike most quantum processors today, which use transmon qubits, Atlantic Quantum is developing systems based on fluxonium qubits. These qubits operate at significantly lower frequencies, which translates into much longer coherence times and simpler control requirements. This design decision positions fluxonium as a superior candidate for scalable, fault-tolerant architectures. The company is focused not just on the qubits themselves, but on the entire hardware stack, including advanced control electronics that can maintain high gate fidelity and expand efficiently as systems grow in size.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company’s hardware innovations are matched by an emphasis on extensibility. Atlantic Quantum’s architecture integrates noise-protected qubits with scalable control schemes, allowing their processors to advance toward real-world deployment. Their control systems are designed to handle the precision demands of quantum gates while being inherently adaptable for much larger systems in the future.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">With a small but highly specialized team, Atlantic Quantum maintains <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/07/18/riverlane-partners-with-atlantic-quantum-to-advance-quantum-error-correction-on-fluxonium-architecture/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">partnerships with three organizations and one university.</a> This reflects a strategy that balances independent innovation with academic and institutional collaboration, ensuring both technical rigor and practical relevance.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/atlantic-quantum/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Atlantic Quantum</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>15. Atom Computing </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="76" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_BFMH-zpiq_tcpME3Kt-qLw-300x76.png" alt="Atom Computing Logo" class="wp-image-2359604" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_BFMH-zpiq_tcpME3Kt-qLw-300x76.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_BFMH-zpiq_tcpME3Kt-qLw.png 445w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://atom-computing.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Atom Computing</a> is a private quantum computing company founded in 2018 that builds scalable quantum systems using nuclear spin qubits formed from optically trapped neutral atoms. Atom Computing is one of the leaders in neutral atom quantum technology, offering long coherence times, high qubit fidelity, and a platform designed for scalability.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Atom Computing’s qubit control approach is wireless and laser-based, enabling precise manipulation of individual atoms that are spaced just a few microns apart. This architectural design allows the company to develop highly scalable quantum processors. Their first-generation system, Phoenix, is a 100-qubit gate-based machine with a universal quantum gate set and a remarkable coherence time of 21 seconds. In October 2023, Atom Computing <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2023/10/24/quantum-startup-atom-computing-first-to-exceed-1000-qubits/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">announced a significant milestone</a>: a 1,225-site atomic array, of which 1,180 sites were populated with working qubits, making it one of the largest quantum systems ever publicly disclosed.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company’s technology is built to be programmable through standard industry platforms, making it compatible with a range of quantum development tools. Atom Computing supports a complete software stack, including libraries, quantum algorithms, and circuit-level access, and it also offers consulting services to help clients leverage its technology for specialized applications.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In a major international expansion, Atom Computing established its European headquarters in Copenhagen, Denmark, as part of a strategic partnership with the Danish government to advance quantum research and industry. Supported by a 70 million DKK investment from Denmark’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs and EIFO, this initiative aligns with Denmark’s National Strategy on Quantum Technology and the QIST collaboration agreement with the United States. The partnership is designed to bring advanced quantum technology and job creation to the region, strengthening ties between Europe and the U.S. in the quantum domain.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/09/11/microsoft-and-atom-computing-partner-to-build-a-quantum-supercomputer/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Atom Computing is also collaborating with Microsoft</a> on building utility-scale, fault-tolerant quantum supercomputers. In September 2024, the two companies announced their joint development of quantum systems based on Atom’s neutral atom hardware. By November 2024, they revealed a commercial quantum system featuring 24 logical qubits ,  the largest number of entangled logical qubits ever demonstrated. Integrated into Microsoft’s Azure Quantum platform, the system achieves significantly lower error rates than physical qubits and is tailored for advanced use cases in chemistry, materials science, and AI. The commercial availability of this system is expected in 2025.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">With a strong partner network that includes 9 enterprise users, 59 organizations, 4 research centers, 1 government, and 13 investors, Atom Computing is at the forefront of building practical, high-performance quantum computers.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/atom-computing" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Atom Computing</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>16. BlueQubit</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="75" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/BQ_logo_new_white_bg-300x75.jpg" alt="BlueQubit Logo" class="wp-image-2380849" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/BQ_logo_new_white_bg-300x75.jpg 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/BQ_logo_new_white_bg-768x193.jpg 768w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/BQ_logo_new_white_bg-1024x257.jpg 1024w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/BQ_logo_new_white_bg-1536x386.jpg 1536w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/BQ_logo_new_white_bg-2048x515.jpg 2048w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.bluequbit.io/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">BlueQubit</a>, founded in 2022, is a quantum software platform focused on quantum machine learning. It provides access to both classical emulators ,  supporting up to 34 qubits ,  and real quantum processing units. The platform is compatible with Qiskit, Cirq, and NVIDIA’s classical infrastructure, enabling flexible quantum-classical hybrid workflows.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company is building a scalable, commercially viable ecosystem for quantum applications across industries. In 2023, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2023/10/26/bluequbits-algorithm-chosen-by-darpa-leveraging-gpu-simulators-and-infrastructure-to-advance-quantum-ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">the company was selected for DARPA’s IMPAQT program</a>, where it is developing AI/ML algorithms optimized for NISQ-era quantum devices.</p>
