<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://quantumbrilliance.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Quantum Brilliance</a>, founded in 2019, develops quantum computing systems based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in synthetic diamond. Unlike traditional quantum platforms, its diamond-based processors operate at room temperature and require only electrical power ,  removing the need for cryogenics or vacuum systems.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company has built a compact two-qubit quantum accelerator designed for deployment in conventional computing environments. Their Qristal SDK enables the development of hybrid classical-quantum applications tailored for edge computing, data centers, and HPC clusters. Use cases span from robotics and satellites to computational chemistry.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Quantum Brilliance’s technology is currently integrated with Setonix, the Pawsey Supercomputing Centre’s flagship HPC system, supporting co-processing tasks. <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/09/18/quantum-brilliance-parityqc-win-german-contract-to-build-a-mobile-quantum-computer-by-2027/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">In 2024, the company partnered with ParityQC</a> and Germany’s Cyber Agency on the €35 million Diamonds-MQC project to build mobile, energy-efficient quantum computers for defense and civilian applications.</p>

(QCI)</a>, founded in 2015 by Yale University physicists Michel Devoret, Luigi Frunzio, and Robert Schoelkopf, is building a full-stack quantum computing platform based on superconducting circuits. <p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/09/03/quantum-circuits-integrates-with-nvidia-cuda-q-to-advance-creation-and-testing-of-first-quantum-applications-based-on-dual-rail-qubits/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">In 2024, the company introduced the Dual-Rail Qubit (DRQ)</a>, which uses two resonators sharing a photon to encode information. QCI also launched its Aqumen Seeker system, featuring eight DRQs in a nearest-neighbor layout, allowing real-time error correction and development of fault-tolerant protocols.</p>

<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2021/04/19/quantum-brilliance-quantum-south-partner-to-implement-logistics-optimization-applications-using-quantum-computing/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">The company has partnered with Quantum Brilliance</a> to integrate NV-center-based quantum systems and is also collaborating with Kipu Quantum to combine application-specific quantum algorithms with its own logistics tools ,  targeting improved performance, security, and profitability for Latin American and global markets.</p>

<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><strong>58. Quantum Source</strong></p>



<figure class="wp-block-image"><img decoding="async" src="https://t26485008.p.clickup-attachments.com/t26485008/6088f8fd-e5ce-4292-8f9f-d7b1678ad2b0/image.png" alt="" width="502" height="200"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Quantum Source is a photonic-quantum computing company focused on deterministic, high-brightness single-photon generation and integrated photonic architectures engineered for large-scale cluster-state computation. The core thesis: improve photon source quality (brightness, purity, indistinguishability) and on-chip integration to unlock practical fault-tolerant photonics without cryogenic ion/superconducting stacks.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company’s stack targets repeatable, clocked photon emission feeding reconfigurable interferometers, fast feed-forward, and loss-tolerant encoding — aimed at scalable error-corrected computation using photonic graph states. Development workflow leans on classical simulation and control co-design to validate architectures and error models prior to fab cycles.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/09/16/quantum-source-unveils-origin-a-noval-core-engine-for-scalable-fault-tolerant-photonic-quantum-computers/">recent news on Quantum Source</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>59. Quantware </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="115" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_iZ6laQrnY9Kej9uSElYF7Q-300x115.png" alt="Quantware Logo" class="wp-image-2359648" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_iZ6laQrnY9Kej9uSElYF7Q-300x115.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_iZ6laQrnY9Kej9uSElYF7Q.png 362w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.quantware.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">QuantWare</a>, founded in 2021, develops and sells superconducting quantum hardware including quantum processing units (QPUs), amplifiers, and cryogenic components. Positioned as a scalable hardware supplier, the company offers configurable QPUs like the Soprano-D and Contralto-D models, featuring up to 25 controllable qubits, designed for fast delivery and customizable configurations, previously accessible only to major tech labs.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Their product line includes Crescendo (a nearly quantum-limited TWPA for low-noise qubit readout), VIO-176 (a 3D chip platform supporting over 100 qubits), and Foundry Services with 45-day turnaround for custom chips. Pricing begins at €60,000 for entry-level QPUs and €300,000 for advanced Contralto units.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In 2024 and 2025, QuantWare expanded its enterprise offerings with Crescendo-E for high-volume TWPA deployment and enabled automated QPU characterization in under 25 minutes via partnerships with QuantrolOx and Qblox ,  a breakthrough in simplifying quantum integration and accelerating development cycles.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/Quantware/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Quantware</a>.</p>

<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Founded in 2018, <a href="https://qubitengineering.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Qubit Engineering</a> is a quantum software company focused on energy sector applications. It develops quantum and classical optimization algorithms, particularly for wind turbine micro-siting ,  helping to improve wind farm efficiency and reduce project costs.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Their flagship product leverages quantum computing to optimize turbine placement, increasing energy output and lowering operational overhead in renewable energy infrastructure.</p>

<figure class="wp-block-image size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="323" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qubit-nouveau-1024x323.png" alt="" class="wp-image-2382166" style="width:251px;height:auto" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qubit-nouveau-1024x323.png 1024w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qubit-nouveau-300x95.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qubit-nouveau-768x242.png 768w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qubit-nouveau-1536x484.png 1536w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qubit-nouveau.png 1700w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.qubit-pharmaceuticals.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Qubit Pharmaceuticals</a>, founded in 2020, is a Paris-based startup developing software platforms to accelerate drug discovery using high-performance computing (HPC) and emerging quantum computing technologies. Their core suite, ATLAS, is designed to simulate and co-design new drug candidates in collaboration with pharmaceutical and biotech firms.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company also offers Gaia, a hybrid platform built on NVIDIA’s Quantum Optimized Device Architecture, which combines classical and quantum computing to dramatically reduce both development time and cost. In addition, their Hyperion simulation platform enables the development of next-generation quantum chemistry algorithms.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In July 2024, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2024/07/10/qubit-pharmaceuticals-and-sorbonne-university-reduce-the-number-of-qubits-needed-to-simulate-molecules/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Qubit Pharmaceuticals announced that its Hyperion-1 emulator</a>, co-developed with Sorbonne Université, reduced the quantum hardware requirements for small molecule simulations. The innovation earned €8 million in funding through the France 2030 national plan, reinforcing the company’s leading role in quantum-enabled drug discovery.</p>

<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>62. QuEra Computing </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-full"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="298" height="145" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_Rt8GmQqUPQtkytcRBQd_8Q.png" alt="Quera Logo" class="wp-image-2359645"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Founded in 2019,<a href="https://www.quera.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow"> QuEra Computing</a> builds scalable, programmable quantum computers based on neutral atom technology developed at Harvard and MIT. Its hardware traps and manipulates atoms using laser beams in vacuum chambers, creating reconfigurable qubit arrays cooled to near absolute zero. This architecture, powered by Rydberg blockade interactions, supports both analog and digital quantum computation.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">QuEra’s Aquila processor, accessible via Amazon Braket, enables up to 256 neutral atom qubits, with dynamic control over qubit arrangement and local detuning, allowing for high flexibility in problem solving and analog simulation. Its roadmap targets 10 logical qubits by 2024, 30 by 2025 with error correction, and 100 by 2026 ,  paving the way toward utility-scale quantum computing.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company has achieved a 99.5% two-qubit gate fidelity and launched tools like Bloqade, an open-source emulator. It is actively developing hybrid digital-analog platforms, and recently introduced a co-design program integrating hardware, software, and applications to accelerate adoption in business and research.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">QuEra has also formed major partnerships, including with Kipu Quantum and PlanQK to provide easier access to quantum technology, and received investment from Google Quantum AI to advance fault-tolerance. In 2024, QuEra launched the Quantum Alliance Partner Program to unite tech leaders in addressing global computational challenges using neutral-atom systems.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/quera-computing/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on QuEra Computing</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>63. QuintessenceLabs </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="141" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/New_QuintessenceLabs_Logo-300x141.jpg" alt="Quintessence Labs Logo" class="wp-image-2380894" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/New_QuintessenceLabs_Logo-300x141.jpg 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/New_QuintessenceLabs_Logo.jpg 695w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.quintessencelabs.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">QuintessenceLabs</a>, founded way back in 2008 out of research from the Australian National University, is a cybersecurity company pioneering quantum-safe encryption technologies. Focused on addressing the vulnerabilities of classical encryption in a post-quantum world, the company offers a range of advanced solutions to secure sensitive data.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Their flagship offerings include qOptica™, a Continuous Variable Quantum Key Distribution (CV-QKD) system enabling secure key exchange over optical links, and a high-speed quantum random number generator that delivers true randomness essential for robust encryption. QuintessenceLabs also provides crypto-agile key management tools and supports the Key Management Interoperability Protocol (KMIP) for seamless integration with enterprise systems.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Recognized globally for innovation, including as a World Economic Forum Global Innovator, QuintessenceLabs is tackling threats such as “harvest now, decrypt later” attacks with scalable, quantum-resilient security infrastructure.</p>

<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>64. QuiX Quantum </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-thumbnail"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="150" height="112" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/quix_quantum_bv_logo-e1757716794619-150x112.jpg" alt="Quix Logo" class="wp-image-2360133"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">A company founded in 2019 and based in the Netherlands, <a href="https://www.quixquantum.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">QuiX Quantum</a> specializes in integrated photonics for optical quantum computing. The company has developed reconfigurable photonic processors using stoichiometric silicon nitride (Si₃N₄) waveguides that operate at room temperature and offer high-precision remote configurability. These processors are designed to interface with a wide range of light sources due to the platform’s broad transparency range.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">QuiX’s photonic processors have achieved significant technical milestones, including the launch of the world’s largest photonic quantum processor in 2022 and the delivery of 8-qubit and 64-qubit photonic quantum computers to the German Aerospace Center (DLR QCI). Their 20-mode Quantum Photonic Processor demonstrates low optical losses (2.9 dB) and transformation fidelity of 99%, supporting complex linear operations on-chip.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company’s product portfolio includes turnkey quantum photonic processors, a scalable photonic quantum computer hardware stack, and customized solutions for sensing, communication, and computing. Notably, QuiX has demonstrated the on-chip generation of Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states ,  an essential step in creating complex entangled systems for scalable quantum computing.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In September 2024, QuiX launched Bia, a photonic-based quantum cloud platform for business and research use, supporting applications such as Boson Sampling and Quantum Monte Carlo simulations. In July 2025, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/07/10/quix-quantum-secures-e15-million-to-deliver-first-generation-universal-photonic-quantum-computer/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">QuiX raised €15 million in Series A funding</a> to accelerate development of the world’s first universal single-photon-based photonic quantum computer, expected in 2026.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/quix-quantum/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on QuiX Quantum</a>.</p>

<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="138" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_xYYusp81t9p5mnIINbpHbQ-300x138.png" alt="QunaSys" class="wp-image-2359726" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_xYYusp81t9p5mnIINbpHbQ-300x138.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_xYYusp81t9p5mnIINbpHbQ.png 331w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://qunasys.com/en/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">QunaSys</a>, founded in 2018 and originating from the universities of Tokyo, Osaka, and Kyoto, develops quantum algorithms for chemistry, healthcare, and materials science. The company is known for maintaining Qulacs, a high-speed quantum circuit simulator written in C++ with a Python interface, widely used in quantum computational research. QunaSys also leads QPARC, a Japanese quantum computing consortium involving over 50 global enterprises focused on industrial applications.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company launched Qamuy, a quantum cloud platform, and is a member of global initiatives like Q-STAR, Les Maisons du Quantique, and the Pistoia Alliance. In May 2023, QunaSys received an investment from IBM Ventures. In 2024, the company released QURI Chemistry on IBM’s Qiskit Functions Catalog, providing quantum algorithms tailored for chemical and materials R&D without requiring deep quantum programming knowledge. That same year, QunaSys introduced QURI SDK, an open-source platform supporting fault-tolerant quantum algorithm design, aiming to expand access to FTQC research across multiple disciplines.</p>

<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>66. Qunnect</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="186" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qunnect-3-1-300x186.webp" alt="Qunnect Logo" class="wp-image-2380896" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qunnect-3-1-300x186.webp 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qunnect-3-1-768x476.webp 768w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/Logo-Qunnect-3-1.webp 793w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.qunnect.inc/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Qunnect</a>, founded in 2017, builds light-based modular devices for quantum encryption, anchored by a room-temperature quantum memory. Its flagship rack-mounted memory device can store, manipulate, and retrieve optical qubits on demand, functioning like a telecom data buffer for quantum networks.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In collaboration with NYU, Qunnect established a 16km quantum link across New York City, demonstrating real-world quantum networking capabilities over standard fiber. The company’s GothamQ system later automated the distribution of entangled photons across 34km of urban fiber, maintaining 99% fidelity and 99.84% uptime ,  showcasing the feasibility of a quantum internet.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In June 2025, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/06/24/qunnect-raises-10-million/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Qunnect raised $10 million in a Series A extension</a> to scale deployment of its Carina product suite, a turnkey entanglement-based quantum networking solution compatible with commercial fiber infrastructure.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/Qunnect/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Qunnect</a>.</p>

<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.qnulabs.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">QNu Labs</a> is an Indian startup delivering quantum key distribution solutions for secure communications. Founded in 2016 and headquartered in Bengaluru, it is India’s first company to develop commercial quantum-based cybersecurity products. Incubated at IIT-Madras, it later expanded to the US with a subsidiary in Massachusetts. Backed by Cisco Launchpad in 2018, QNu launched Armos (quantum key distribution) and Tropos (quantum random number generator) in 2020 for global markets.</p>

<figure class="wp-block-image size-thumbnail"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="150" height="97" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_Uyw7VaVxOhNgxELOt4cO3g-e1757716763226-150x97.jpg" alt="Riverlane Logo" class="wp-image-2359727"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.riverlane.com/">Riverlane</a>, founded in 2016, is a UK-based global leader in quantum error correction (QEC) and is solely focused on solving error correction. Its flagship platform, Deltaflow, includes tools for control, calibration, runtime deployment, and decoding, all aimed at supporting real-time QEC.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Riverlane has developed a quantum simulation engine and is building a universal operating system for quantum computers. Key projects include Deltaflow, Riverlane’s quantum error correction stack with real-time QEC decoding, and Deltakit, the company’s education and tooling resources platform for learning, developing and adopting real-time QEC.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Backed by the University of Cambridge, leading technology VC firms, Horizon Europe and DSIT, Riverlane collaborates with companies like Rigetti and research institutions including Oak Ridge National Lab, achieving microsecond-scale error correction on real hardware. Its roadmap aims to support logical Clifford operations on systems with up to 1,000 physical qubits by 2025.</p>

<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.scalinq.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">SCALINQ</a>, founded in 2022, provides scalable hardware solutions for quantum computers, with a focus on superconducting and spin-qubit platforms.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company offers full-stack packaging, filtering, and wiring products designed to simplify quantum processor scaling. Its flagship product, LINQER600, developed with IQM, is the largest commercial qubit packaging solution, capable of supporting chips with hundreds of qubits.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Tested with Chalmers University’s 150-qubit Radiance chip, LINQER600 is fully non-magnetic and tailored for high-density, large-scale quantum computing development.</p>

<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://seeqc.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">SeeQC</a>, founded in 2019, is developing a fully digital quantum computing platform based on superconducting technology. Its unique Digital Quantum Computing architecture integrates quantum processors with Single Flux Quantum (SFQ) digital chips that operate at cryogenic temperatures, enabling high-speed, energy-efficient control directly alongside qubits.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Their SeeQC System Red demonstrates this capability, delivering two-qubit gate speeds of 39ns and fidelities of 98.4% ,  among the fastest available via public quantum cloud services. Built using silicon wafers at their in-house fab, SeeQC’s platform aims to overcome the inefficiencies and scaling limits of analog architectures by merging classical and quantum co-processing on a single chip.</p>

<p class="wp-block-paragraph">These detectors are used in fields like quantum communication, quantum key distribution (QKD), LiDAR, and bio-imaging, and are among the fastest single-photon counting devices available. <p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.spinquanta.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">SpinQ</a>, founded in 2018, develops quantum computing hardware and software, including superconducting and desktop NMR-based quantum systems. Their offerings span education, scientific research, AI, finance, and drug discovery.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company provides cloud access to its superconducting computer, Lyra, and three NMR desktop systems: Gemini, Gemini Mini, and Triangulum. These systems support up to 8 qubits via Taurus Cloud, with programming through graphical interfaces, QASM, and Python-based SpinQKit.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In 2024, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2023/12/14/spinq-ships-superconducting-quantum-chip-to-middle-east-client/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">SpinQ launched a 20-qubit superconducting chip </a>and the Vega quantum measurement system. Their web-based Tianyi EDA tool enables rapid design and simulation of superconducting chips. In August, they introduced SPINQ Gemini Lab, a 2-qubit NMR platform for hands-on teaching and quantum algorithm experimentation in European and North American universities.</p>

<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>73. Silicon Quantum Computing</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="118" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_60eYTM5iys75_7KBKvU87g-300x118.png" alt="Silicon QC Logo" class="wp-image-2359658" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_60eYTM5iys75_7KBKvU87g-300x118.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_60eYTM5iys75_7KBKvU87g.png 357w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="http://www.sqc.com.au/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC)</a> is a full-stack quantum computing company headquartered in Sydney, Australia. Founded in 2017 by world-leading materials scientist, Michelle Simmons, the company develops quantum computers and application-specific systems using precision-placed atoms in silicon: an approach within the field of spin qubits. </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">SQC is the only private quantum computing company that manufactures its own quantum chips. The company’s proprietary PAQMan™ (Precision Atom Qubit Manufacturing) process, developed over 25 years, patterns chips with 0.13 nanometer precision, placing individual phosphorus atoms within pure silicon wafers. It is the most accurate semiconductor manufacturing process in the world.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">As a full-stack company, SQC can design, produce and test new quantum chips in under one week: an execution speed that sets the company apart in the global quantum computing race. In February 2025, SQC demonstrated up to <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/02/20/silicon-quantum-computing-executes-high-fidelity-grovers-algorithm-without-qec-but-scaling-challenges-remain/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">98.9% accuracy in the operation of Grover’s algorithm</a> without error correction. In December 2025, <em>Nature</em> published results from SQC’s multi-qubit, multi-register processor achieved <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/12/17/sqc-study-shows-silicon-based-quantum-processor-can-scale-without-loss-of-fidelity/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">qubit fidelities up to 99.99%</a>, demonstrating increasing qubit quality with increasing qubit counts.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">SQC has two products in market: Watermelon™, a quantum-enhanced AI system, and Quantum Twins™, a quantum simulator designed to accelerate molecule and materials discovery. These products have already delivered commercial outcomes across a variety of sectors including energy, finance, defense and telecommunications, with its customer Telstra reporting <a href="https://www.telstra.com.au/aboutus/media/media-releases/Telstra-SQC-quantum-network" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">dramatic reductions in model training times</a> using the Watermelon processor.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read SQC’s most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/silicon-quantum-computing/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Silicon Quantum Computing</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>74. Sparrow Quantum</strong></h4>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://sparrowquantum.com/">Sparrow Quantum</a>, founded in 2015 as a spin-off from the Niels Bohr Institute, specializes in high-performance single-photon sources based on InAs quantum dots and photonic-crystal waveguides. Their technology achieves photon coherence indistinguishability between 95% and 98%.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In October 2024, Sparrow’s photon chip was integrated into ORCA Computing’s PT-2 quantum system, advancing generative AI and quantum machine learning through scalable, HPC-compatible photonic quantum solutions.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/sparrow-quantum/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Sparrow Quantum</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>75. Strangeworks </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="157" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_0blSo3Gd6SNroCWcxz2ikQ-300x157.png" alt="Strange works Logo" class="wp-image-2359729" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_0blSo3Gd6SNroCWcxz2ikQ-300x157.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_0blSo3Gd6SNroCWcxz2ikQ.png 310w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://strangeworks.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Strangeworks</a>, founded in 2018, is a quantum computing software company focused on making quantum and emerging computing technologies accessible. Serving sectors like aerospace, energy, finance, and pharmaceuticals, the company provides tools that integrate quantum, AI, and high-performance computing.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Its flagship offering, Strangeworks QC, is a free platform that supports quantum programming using tools like Qiskit, Cirq, and Amazon Braket. For enterprise users, Strangeworks EQ delivers infrastructure with IP protection, private deployments, and training. The company also runs the Strangeworks Quantum Syndicate, a collaborative community uniting hardware and software providers, educators, and developers to accelerate the future of quantum computing.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/Strangeworks/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Strangeworks</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>76. Terra Quantum </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-thumbnail"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="150" height="150" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_5v7rReC0a4YbOcOX4CamUw-150x150.png" alt="Terra Quantum Logo" class="wp-image-2359730"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Founded in 2019, <a href="https://terraquantum.swiss/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Terra Quantum</a> AG is based in Switzerland and develops quantum software, hardware, and algorithms across cybersecurity, AI/ML, and life sciences. Its operations are structured around three core services: Quantum Algorithms as a Service, Quantum Compute as a Service, and Quantum Safety as a Service.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Their offerings include TQ42, a hybrid quantum cloud platform for enterprises, and TQ42 Cryptography, an open-source library aligned with NIST’s post-quantum standards. Terra Quantum has achieved a 1707 km quantum key distribution (QKD) milestone, and their QCKD protocol enables secure communication over long distances.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In 2024, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2023/12/16/terra-quantum-launches-tq42-quantum-as-a-service-platform/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">the company launched the TQ42 Studio </a>,  a hybrid AI/quantum development platform ,  and began a collaboration with Unilever using quantum machine learning for skin microbiome research. Terra Quantum is also working with the U.S. Air Force under an SBIR contract to develop a quantum-resistant communication network.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/Terra-Quantum/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Terra Quantum</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>77. Turing</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="94" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_tSpFmILx8EX8nCPZyw7l0g-300x94.png" alt="Turing Logo" class="wp-image-2359659" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_tSpFmILx8EX8nCPZyw7l0g-300x94.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_tSpFmILx8EX8nCPZyw7l0g.png 401w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.turing.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Turing</a>, founded in 2016, is a private AI software company focused on blending classical and quantum machine learning to build practical, socially beneficial quantum applications. The company is currently developing quantum random access memory (qRAM) to enable scalable, fault-tolerant quantum computing. Their qRAM approach centers on storing and producing entanglement efficiently ,  key to scaling quantum machines for industrial use.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/Turing/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Turing</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>78. Universal Quantum </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-thumbnail"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="150" height="150" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_aAZMGbLw5nw0OmA12OCJwQ-150x150.jpg" alt="Universal Quantum Logo" class="wp-image-2359660" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_aAZMGbLw5nw0OmA12OCJwQ-150x150.jpg 150w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_aAZMGbLw5nw0OmA12OCJwQ.jpg 200w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 150px) 100vw, 150px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://universalquantum.com/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Universal Quantum</a>, founded in 2019, is developing scalable trapped-ion quantum computers using microwave-based technology instead of laser beams. This design lowers the cooling requirements to -200°C, making the system more practical and scalable. In a major milestone, Universal Quantum secured a €67 million contract from the German Aerospace Center (DLR) to build two quantum computers ,  one single-chip and one multi-chip system with up to 100 qubits ,  within four years.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company, in collaboration with the University of Sussex, demonstrated record-breaking qubit transfer speeds and connectivity by linking quantum microchips with 99.999993% fidelity. In 2024, they launched the first commercial ASIC chip for their iQPU, improving qubit control and error correction, and introduced new methods such as transversal CNOT gates and constant-time magic state distillation to enhance fault tolerance and scalability. Universal Quantum ultimately aims to build machines capable of handling millions of qubits.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/Universal-Quantum/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Universal Quantum</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>79. Welinq</strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="123" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/welinq-logo-noirblanc-768x432-1-e1757716621975-300x123.png" alt="Weling Logo" class="wp-image-2380901" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/welinq-logo-noirblanc-768x432-1-e1757716621975-300x123.png 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/09/welinq-logo-noirblanc-768x432-1-e1757716621975.png 394w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Founded in 2022, <a href="https://www.welinq.fr/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Welinq</a> develops hardware solutions to interconnect quantum processors over long distances without losing coherence. Its technology is centered on high-performance quantum memory based on cold atoms, enabling the creation of quantum links that scale computing power by connecting multiple QPUs. Supported by academic partners like Sorbonne Université and CNRS, Welinq’s R&D is based in Paris.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In March 2025, the company launched the first commercial quantum memory for data centers, achieving over 90% efficiency at room temperature. This breakthrough positions Welinq as a key enabler of distributed quantum architectures and the future quantum internet.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/Welinq/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Welinq</a>.</p>



<h4 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>80. Xanadu </strong></h4>



<figure class="wp-block-image size-medium"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="300" height="134" src="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_wcEtdU7JOPk-P3-PAihc_g-300x134.jpg" alt="Xanadu Logo" class="wp-image-2359661" srcset="https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_wcEtdU7JOPk-P3-PAihc_g-300x134.jpg 300w, https://thequantuminsider.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/1_wcEtdU7JOPk-P3-PAihc_g.jpg 336w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 300px) 100vw, 300px"></figure>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><a href="https://www.xanadu.ai/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener nofollow">Xanadu</a>, founded in 2016, is a leading full-stack quantum computing company focused on photonics. It develops both hardware and software, including the open-source platforms Strawberry Fields and PennyLane, enabling quantum machine learning and hybrid quantum-classical computing. Its hardware innovations are based on silicon photonic chips that operate at room temperature.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The company’s flagship system, Borealis, is a 216-qubit photonic quantum computer that demonstrated quantum advantage in 2022. Available via Xanadu Cloud and Amazon Braket, Borealis showcases squeezed-state qubits for specialized quantum tasks. In 2025, <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/2025/01/22/xanadu-announces-aurora-a-universal-photonic-quantum-computer/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">Xanadu introduced Aurora</a>, a scalable, modular photonic quantum computer designed for fault tolerance, with ambitions to launch a full-scale quantum data center by 2029.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Xanadu is also advancing quantum error correction through the development of Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) qubits ,  error-resistant photonic states that enable reliable quantum operations at room temperature, addressing two of the field’s biggest challenges: scalability and error correction.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Read our most recent <a href="https://thequantuminsider.com/tag/Xanadu/" target="_blank" rel="noreferrer noopener">news on Xanadu</a>.</p>



<h2 class="wp-block-heading"><strong>Conclusion</strong></h2>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">The quantum computing sector in 2026 is a dynamic fusion of established tech giants and agile innovators, each pushing the boundaries of what’s computationally possible.</p>



<h2 class="wp-block-heading">Frequently Asked Questions</h2>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><strong>What is quantum advantage, and why does it matter?</strong> </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Quantum advantage refers to the tipping point where quantum machines usefully outperform their classical counterparts. Companies across the quantum ecosystem are working toward this milestone, as it would unlock solutions to problems currently out of reach for classical systems.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><strong>Which major tech companies are leading quantum computing development?</strong> </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">IBM, Google, Microsoft, and AWS are among the leading big tech players, each investing significantly in quantum R&D. IBM operates the world’s largest fleet of quantum systems through its Quantum Computation Center in New York, while Google achieved a landmark with its 105-qubit Willow chip.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><strong>What qubit technologies are companies currently pursuing?</strong> </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Companies are exploring diverse qubit modalities including superconducting qubits (IBM, Rigetti), trapped ions (IonQ, Quantinuum), neutral atoms (PASQAL, QuEra), photonic qubits (Xanadu, PsiQuantum), and silicon spin qubits (Diraq, Quantum Motion). Each approach offers different trade-offs in coherence time, error rates, and scalability.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><strong>What is AWS’s first proprietary quantum chip, and what makes it unique?</strong> </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">In February 2025, AWS unveiled Ocelot, its first proprietary quantum chip, developed at the AWS Center for Quantum Computing at Caltech. It is built with cat qubits that suppress environmental noise and reduce error-correction overhead, using an architecture of 14 physical qubits including buffers and detection circuits.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><strong>How is the quantum computing industry addressing error correction?</strong> </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Error correction is a central challenge, with companies like Riverlane building dedicated quantum error correction platforms and Google demonstrating with its Willow chip that increasing qubit numbers can actually reduce errors. Other firms like Alice & Bob use cat qubits and Nord Quantique uses bosonic codes to suppress errors directly at the qubit level.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><strong>Are quantum computers only accessible via specialized hardware on-site?</strong> </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">No — many quantum systems are accessible via cloud platforms such as IBM Quantum, Amazon Braket, Azure Quantum, and IonQ Quantum Cloud, allowing researchers and enterprises to run algorithms without managing physical hardware. Some companies like Anyon Systems and IQM also offer on-premise systems tailored for HPC centers and government clients.</p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph"><strong>What industries are quantum computing companies targeting?</strong> </p>



<p class="wp-block-paragraph">Quantum computing applications span healthcare, finance, materials science, logistics, cryptography, drug discovery, and aerospace, among others. Companies like Multiverse Computing focus on finance and energy, while firms like ProteinQure and Qubit Pharmaceuticals target pharmaceutical R&D and drug discovery.<br></p>

<script id="tqi-cards-nohover">
(function () {
	function stop(e) {
		if (e.target.closest && (e.target.closest('.tqi-grid') || e.target.closest('.tqi-card'))) {
			e.stopPropagation();
		}
	}
	['mouseover', 'mouseout', 'mousemove', 'mouseenter', 'mouseleave'].forEach(function (evt) {
		document.addEventListener(evt, stop, true); /* capture phase: intercept before document handlers */
	});
})();
</script>

    var gatedPDFs = {"https:\/\/thequantuminsider.com\/locked\/?return_url=https%3A%2F%2Fthequantuminsider.com%2Fwp-content%2Fuploads%2F2025%2F07%2FTQI-2025-Q2-vUpdatedFinance-USE.pdf&post_id=2379433":2379433,"https:\/\/thequantuminsider.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2025\/04\/TQI-Quarterly-Report-Q1-2025-Official.pdf":2377535,"https:\/\/thequantuminsider.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2025\/03\/2025-02-27-Quantum-Market-Map.pdf":2376635,"https:\/\/thequantuminsider.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2025\/03\/03.03.2025-Extracted-Quantum-QKD-report-.pdf":2376316};
    var gpa_enable_cookies = true;

    $('a').each(function() {
        var linkHref = $(this).attr('href');
        if (gatedPDFs.hasOwnProperty(linkHref)) {
            $(this).on('click', function(e) {
                var pdfId = gatedPDFs[linkHref];
                if (gpa_enable_cookies && document.cookie.indexOf('pdf_access_' + pdfId + '=1') !== -1) {
                    return true;
                }
                e.preventDefault();
                if (typeof PUM !== 'undefined') {
                    PUM.open(2376276);
                }
                $('#popup-pdf-redirect').attr('data-pdf-id', pdfId).data('pdf-url', linkHref);
            });
        }
    });

    function checkHubspotResponse() {
        var found = false;
        $('.submitted-message').each(function() {
            if ($(this).text().indexOf("Thank you for filling the form, you will be redirected to a document.") !== -1) {
                found = true;
            }
        });
        if (found) {
            var pdfId = $('#popup-pdf-redirect').attr('data-pdf-id');
            var pdfUrl = $('#popup-pdf-redirect').data('pdf-url');
            if (pdfId && pdfUrl) {
                if (gpa_enable_cookies) {
                    document.cookie = "pdf_access_" + pdfId + "=1; path=/; max-age=" + (60*60*24*30);
                }
                setTimeout(function(){
                    window.location.href = pdfUrl;
                }, 2000);
            }
        }
    }

    setInterval(checkHubspotResponse, 2000);
});
//# sourceURL=gpa-inline-script-js-after
</script>

<script id="tqi-profile-click-beacon">
(function () {
	"use strict";
	var CFG = {
		ajax:   "https:\/\/thequantuminsider.com\/wp-admin\/admin-ajax.php",
		nonce:  "1afe785c3a",
		postId: 2380848,
		title:  "Quantum Computing Companies in 2026: Mapping the Global Quantum Landscape",
		action: "tqi_profile_click"	};

	function companyFromCard(btn) {
		var n = btn.getAttribute('data-tqi-name');
		if (n) return n.trim();
		var card = btn.closest('#tqi-hovercard, .tqi-hovercard, #tqi-card') ||
		           document.getElementById('tqi-hovercard');
		if (card) {
			var nameEl = card.querySelector('#tqi-card-name, .tqi-card-name, #tqi-card-title');
			if (nameEl && nameEl.textContent) return nameEl.textContent.trim();
		}
		return btn.getAttribute('href') || '';
	}

	function send(btn) {
		var body = new URLSearchParams();
		body.set('action',  CFG.action);
		body.set('nonce',   CFG.nonce);
		body.set('post_id', CFG.postId);
		body.set('title',   CFG.title);
		body.set('company', companyFromCard(btn).slice(0, 191));
		try {
			if (navigator.sendBeacon) { navigator.sendBeacon(CFG.ajax, body); return; }
		} catch (e) {}
		try {
			fetch(CFG.ajax, { method: 'POST', body: body, keepalive: true, credentials: 'same-origin' });
		} catch (e) {}
	}

	document.addEventListener('click', function (ev) {
		var btn = ev.target && ev.target.closest
			? ev.target.closest('.tqi-card-btn-primary') : null;
		if (btn) send(btn);
	}, true);
})();
</script>
		<script id="tqi-hide-empty">
(function () {
	function hideEmpty() {
		document.querySelectorAll('.tqicontain').forEach(function (box) {
			// Did any TQI shortcode actually render content inside this container?
			var hasContent = box.querySelector('.tqi-card, .tqi-grid');

			// A .tqi-grid is only "real" if it has at least one row.
			if (hasContent && hasContent.classList.contains('tqi-grid')) {
				if (!hasContent.querySelector('.tqi-grid-item')) {
					hasContent = null;
				}
			}

			if (!hasContent) {
				box.style.display = 'none';
			}
		});
	}

	if (document.readyState === 'loading') {
		document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', hideEmpty);
	} else {
		hideEmpty();
	}
})();
</script>

                <div class="lcn-pref__name">Analytics</div>
                <div class="lcn-pref__hint">Helps us measure traffic and improve the site.</div>
              </div>
              <label class="lcn-switch" for="lcn-analytics">
                <input id="lcn-analytics" type="checkbox">
                <span class="lcn-switch__pill" aria-hidden="true"></span>
                <span class="lcn-switch__label">Allow</span>
              </label>
            </div>

            <div class="lcn-pref__row">
              <div class="lcn-pref__text">
                <div class="lcn-pref__name">Marketing</div>
                <div class="lcn-pref__hint">Used to personalize content/ads across platforms.</div>
              </div>
              <label class="lcn-switch" for="lcn-marketing">
                <input id="lcn-marketing" type="checkbox">
                <span class="lcn-switch__pill" aria-hidden="true"></span>
                <span class="lcn-switch__label">Allow</span>
              </label>
            </div>
          </div>
        </div>

        <div class="lcn-modal__footer">
          <button class="lcn-btn lcn-btn-ghost" id="lcn-reject" type="button">
            Reject non-essential
          </button>
          <button class="lcn-btn lcn-btn-ghost" id="lcn-accept" type="button">
            Accept all
          </button>
          <button class="lcn-btn lcn-btn-primary" id="lcn-save" type="button">
            OK
          </button>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>

    <style>
      :root{
        --lcn-blue:#1f5fbf;
        --lcn-text:#5b6b7a;
        --lcn-bg:#fff;
        --lcn-border:rgba(0,0,0,.08);
        --lcn-shadow: 0 12px 40px rgba(0,0,0,.18);
        --lcn-radius: 10px;
      }

      /* ===== Banner ===== */
      .lcn-banner{
        position:fixed;
        left:0; right:0; bottom:0;
        z-index:999999;
        background:var(--lcn-bg);
        border-top:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
      }
      .lcn-inner{
        max-width:1200px;
        margin:0 auto;
        padding:16px 20px max(16px, env(safe-area-inset-bottom));
        display:flex;
        align-items:center;
        justify-content:space-between;
        gap:20px;
      }
      .lcn-text{
        flex:1;
        font-size:17px;
        line-height:1.45;
        color:var(--lcn-text);
      }
      .lcn-actions{
        display:flex;
        align-items:center;
        gap:12px;
        flex-shrink:0;
      }
      .lcn-btn{
        font:inherit;
        font-size:15px;
        line-height:1.2;
        padding:8px 14px;
        border-radius:6px;
        cursor:pointer;
        text-decoration:none;
        white-space:nowrap;
        display:inline-flex;
        align-items:center;
        justify-content:center;
        border:1px solid transparent;
        background:#fff;
      }
      .lcn-btn:focus{ outline: 2px solid rgba(31,95,191,.35); outline-offset: 2px; }
      .lcn-btn-primary{
        background:var(--lcn-blue);
        border-color:var(--lcn-blue);
        color:#fff;
      }
      .lcn-btn-ghost{
        background:#fff;
        border-color:var(--lcn-blue);
        color:var(--lcn-blue);
      }

      /* ===== Modal ===== */
      .lcn-modal{
        position:fixed;
        inset:0;
        z-index:1000000;
        display:none;
      }
      .lcn-modal.is-open{ display:block; }
      .lcn-modal__backdrop{
        position:absolute;
        inset:0;
        background:rgba(0,0,0,.45);
      }
      .lcn-modal__panel{
        position:relative;
        width:min(720px, calc(100% - 32px));
        margin: clamp(16px, 6vh, 64px) auto 16px;
        background:var(--lcn-bg);
        border:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
        border-radius:var(--lcn-radius);
        box-shadow:var(--lcn-shadow);
        overflow:hidden;
      }
      .lcn-modal__header{
        padding:16px 18px;
        display:flex;
        align-items:center;
        justify-content:space-between;
        gap:10px;
        border-bottom:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
      }
      .lcn-modal__title{
        margin:0;
        font-size:18px;
        color:#1b2a3a;
      }
      .lcn-icon-btn{
        width:36px; height:36px;
        border-radius:8px;
        border:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
        background:#fff;
        cursor:pointer;
        font-size:22px;
        line-height:1;
        display:flex;
        align-items:center;
        justify-content:center;
        color:#1b2a3a;
      }
      .lcn-modal__body{
        padding:16px 18px;
      }
      .lcn-modal__desc{
        margin:0 0 14px;
        color:var(--lcn-text);
        line-height:1.5;
        font-size:15px;
      }
      .lcn-link{
        color:var(--lcn-blue);
        text-decoration:underline;
      }

      .lcn-pref{
        border:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
        border-radius:12px;
        overflow:hidden;
      }
      .lcn-pref__row{
        display:flex;
        align-items:center;
        justify-content:space-between;
        gap:16px;
        padding:14px 14px;
        border-top:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
      }
      .lcn-pref__row:first-child{ border-top:none; }
      .lcn-pref__name{
        font-weight:600;
        color:#1b2a3a;
        font-size:15px;
      }
      .lcn-pref__hint{
        margin-top:2px;
        color:var(--lcn-text);
        font-size:13px;
        line-height:1.35;
      }

      /* Switch */
      .lcn-switch{
        display:inline-flex;
        align-items:center;
        gap:10px;
        cursor:pointer;
        user-select:none;
        font-size:13px;
        color:#1b2a3a;
      }
      .lcn-switch input{
        position:absolute;
        opacity:0;
        pointer-events:none;
      }
      .lcn-switch__pill{
        width:46px;
        height:26px;
        border-radius:999px;
        border:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
        background:#eef2f6;
        position:relative;
        flex:0 0 auto;
      }
      .lcn-switch__pill::after{
        content:"";
        position:absolute;
        top:50%;
        transform:translateY(-50%);
        left:3px;
        width:20px; height:20px;
        border-radius:50%;
        background:#fff;
        border:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
        transition: left .18s ease, background .18s ease;
      }
      .lcn-switch input:checked + .lcn-switch__pill{
        background:rgba(31,95,191,.14);
        border-color:rgba(31,95,191,.35);
      }
      .lcn-switch input:checked + .lcn-switch__pill::after{
        left:22px;
        border-color:rgba(31,95,191,.35);
      }
      .lcn-switch--locked{
        cursor:default;
        opacity:.9;
      }
      .lcn-switch__pill--on{
        background:rgba(31,95,191,.14);
        border-color:rgba(31,95,191,.35);
      }
      .lcn-switch__pill--on::after{
        left:22px;
        border-color:rgba(31,95,191,.35);
      }

      .lcn-modal__footer{
        padding:14px 18px;
        display:flex;
        gap:10px;
        justify-content:flex-end;
        border-top:1px solid var(--lcn-border);
        flex-wrap:wrap;
      }

      /* ===== Mobile ===== */
      @media (max-width:768px){
        .lcn-inner{
          flex-direction:column;
          align-items:center;
          text-align:center;
          gap:12px;
          padding:14px 16px max(14px, env(safe-area-inset-bottom));
        }
        .lcn-actions{ justify-content:center; flex-wrap:wrap; }
        .lcn-modal__panel{ width: calc(100% - 24px); }
        .lcn-pref__row{ align-items:flex-start; }
      }
      @media (max-width:360px){
        .lcn-text{ font-size:16px; }
        .lcn-btn{ font-size:14px; padding:7px 10px; }
      }
    </style>

    <script>
      (function(){
        var banner = document.getElementById('lcn-banner');
        var modal  = document.getElementById('lcn-modal');
        var manageBtn = document.getElementById('lcn-manage');
        var acceptAllBtnBanner = document.getElementById('lcn-accept-all');

        var chkAnalytics = document.getElementById('lcn-analytics');
        var chkMarketing = document.getElementById('lcn-marketing');

        var btnReject = document.getElementById('lcn-reject');
        var btnAccept = document.getElementById('lcn-accept');
        var btnSave   = document.getElementById('lcn-save');

        if (!banner || !modal) return;

        var lastFocus = null;

        function secureFlag(){
          return (location.protocol === 'https:') ? '; Secure' : '';
        }

        function setConsentCookie(obj){
          try{
            var maxAge = 180 * 86400;
            var value = encodeURIComponent(JSON.stringify(obj));
            document.cookie =
              "lcn_consent=" + value +
              "; Max-Age=" + maxAge +
              "; Path=/; SameSite=Lax" + secureFlag();
          }catch(e){}
        }

        function getDefaultConsent(){
          return {
            v: 1,
            necessary: 1,
            analytics: 0,
            marketing: 0,
            ts: new Date().toISOString()
          };
        }

        function openModal(){
          lastFocus = document.activeElement;
          modal.classList.add('is-open');
          modal.setAttribute('aria-hidden','false');
          document.documentElement.style.overflow = 'hidden';
          // focus first interactive element
          setTimeout(function(){ (chkAnalytics || modal).focus && chkAnalytics.focus(); }, 0);
        }

        function closeModal(){
          modal.classList.remove('is-open');
          modal.setAttribute('aria-hidden','true');
          document.documentElement.style.overflow = '';
          if (lastFocus && lastFocus.focus) lastFocus.focus();
        }

        function saveFromToggles(){
          var consent = getDefaultConsent();
          consent.analytics = chkAnalytics && chkAnalytics.checked ? 1 : 0;
          consent.marketing = chkMarketing && chkMarketing.checked ? 1 : 0;
          setConsentCookie(consent);
          // remove UI
          closeModal();
          if (banner) banner.remove();
        }

        function rejectNonEssential(){
          if (chkAnalytics) chkAnalytics.checked = false;
          if (chkMarketing) chkMarketing.checked = false;
          saveFromToggles();
        }

        function acceptAll(){
          if (chkAnalytics) chkAnalytics.checked = true;
          if (chkMarketing) chkMarketing.checked = true;
          saveFromToggles();
        }

        // Banner buttons
        manageBtn.addEventListener('click', openModal);

        // Banner OK = Accept all (simple UX) + still offers manage cookies
        acceptAllBtnBanner.addEventListener('click', function(){
          acceptAll();
        });

        // Modal buttons
        btnReject.addEventListener('click', rejectNonEssential);
        btnAccept.addEventListener('click', acceptAll);
        btnSave.addEventListener('click', saveFromToggles);

        // Close controls (X / backdrop)
        modal.addEventListener('click', function(e){
          var t = e.target;
          if (t && t.getAttribute && t.getAttribute('data-lcn-close') === '1') {
            closeModal();
          }
        });

        // ESC to close
        document.addEventListener('keydown', function(e){
          if (!modal.classList.contains('is-open')) return;
          if (e.key === 'Escape') closeModal();
        });

        // Minimal focus trap
        document.addEventListener('keydown', function(e){
          if (!modal.classList.contains('is-open')) return;
          if (e.key !== 'Tab') return;

          var focusables = modal.querySelectorAll('button,[href],input,[tabindex]:not([tabindex="-1"])');
          if (!focusables.length) return;

          var first = focusables[0];
          var last  = focusables[focusables.length - 1];

          if (e.shiftKey && document.activeElement === first){
            e.preventDefault(); last.focus();
          } else if (!e.shiftKey && document.activeElement === last){
            e.preventDefault(); first.focus();
          }
        });
      })();
    </script>
